coredata.dc:title: Cobalt-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for the colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode detection of gallic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Congcong
Yin, Yu
Huang, Chongyang
Dong, Yaolin
Liu, Jinquan
Xiao, Fubing
Yang, Shengyuan
coredata.dc:description: Gallic acid (GA), widely used in food and medicine production industries. Herein, a kind of newly synthesized cobalt-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Co,N-CDs) with dual functional properties of oxidase-like activity and photoluminescence property could oxidize the 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to oxTMB with a new absorption peak at 653 nm. In addition, it was found that oxTMB performed a fluorescence-quenching effect on Co,N-CDs at 440 nm attributed to FRET (Fluorescence resonance energy transfer). Reductive GA could reduce oxTMB to TMB, weaken the blue color, and restore the fluorescence of Co,N-CDs. Thus, a colorimetric and fluorometric method for dual-mode detection of GA was established. The synthesis of the dual-signal probe was completed with a single and environmental precursor in just one step. Simultaneously, the dual-mode detection reduced detection limits, broadened the linear range and improved anti-interference ability. This method exhibited a satisfactory application prospect in the actual detection of GA in tea beverages.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
Colorimetric
Fluorometric
Dual-mode
Gallic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of GA in tea beverages
Study on the oxidase-like activity of Co,N-CDs
Standard curves, detection limits and precision

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82073604 , 21906077 ), Postgraduate Research and Innovation Project of Hunan Province ( CX20210962 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS images of Co,N-CDs (A-E): survey scan spectrum (A), high-resolution spectra of C 1 s (C), O 1 s (B), N 1 s (D) and Co 2p (E); FT-IR spectra (F) of Co,N-CDs; UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of Co,N-CDs (G); emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of Co,N-CDs (H).

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oxTMB (curve a), excitation spectrum of Co,N-CDs (curve b) and emission spectrum (curve c) of Co,N-CDs. (B) Fluorescence lifetime diagrams of Co,N-CDs, Co,N-CDs + oxTMB.
Spectra (A) and standard curve (B) for colorimetric detection of GA; Spectra (C) and standard curve (D) for fluorometric detection of GA.
The influence of interfering substances on the GA detection: Colorimetric (A), Fluorometric (B).
Schematic diagram of colorimetric/fluorometric dual-signal detection of GA.
Detection results of GA in tea beverage (n = 7).

head.ce:title: Cobalt-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for the colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode detection of gallic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang, Hunan 421001, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Changzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213002, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Changzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Changzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Changzhou, Jiangsu 213002, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new approach for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal detection of GA in food was established in one step based on cobalt-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with dual functional properties of oxidase-like activity and photoluminescence property.

Gallic acid (GA), widely used in food and medicine production industries. Herein, a kind of newly synthesized cobalt-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Co,N-CDs) with dual functional properties of oxidase-like activity and photoluminescence property could oxidize the 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to oxTMB with a new absorption peak at 653 nm. In addition, it was found that oxTMB performed a fluorescence-quenching effect on Co,N-CDs at 440 nm attributed to FRET (Fluorescence resonance energy transfer). Reductive GA could reduce oxTMB to TMB, weaken the blue color, and restore the fluorescence of Co,N-CDs. Thus, a colorimetric and fluorometric method for dual-mode detection of GA was established. The synthesis of the dual-signal probe was completed with a single and environmental precursor in just one step. Simultaneously, the dual-mode detection reduced detection limits, broadened the linear range and improved anti-interference ability. This method exhibited a satisfactory application prospect in the actual detection of GA in tea beverages.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Gallic acid (GA), also known as 3,4,5-trihydroxybenzoic acid, is a natural phenolic compound presented in a variety of plants, fruits, and tea [1]. As a natural food antioxidant, GA is considered to be healthier than synthetic antioxidants and thus more popular [2,3]. In addition, researchers have pointed out that GA exhibits a variety of beneficial biological activities, such as scavenging free radicals, antihistamine, antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, preventing cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases, and inducing apoptosis of tumor cells [4–7]. Therefore, it is also often used as a dietary supplement in health food production industry. Unfortunately, researchers found that GA has toxic effects on hepatocytes, which undoubtedly leads to potential health effects from its abuse in food and health products [8]. Considering the aforementioned properties, the establishment of a simple and efficient GA analysis is of key significance for the evaluation of food safety, drug efficacy and human health. At present, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)[9], electrochemistry [10], fluorescence [11], colorimetry [7] and chemiluminescence [12] are mainly used to detect GA. However, despite the excellent capability of these methods, their applications are limited by the certain factors, such as the high requirements of chromatography on instruments cannot meet the analysis and detection needs of grass-roots laboratories, electrochemistry and chemiluminescence have poor selectivity, fluorescence are susceptible to photobleaching and light source fluctuations, and the sensitivity of colorimetry is not satisfactory and so on [13–15].

Dual-mode sensor adopting different signals to independently measure the same target can address the inherent limitations of the method and achieve complementary advantages, thereby achieving lessened background interference, improving selectivity, and affording more reliable results [13,16,17]. Recently, researchers have developed a variety of dual-signal probes, such as colorimetric and photothermal modes [18], colorimetric and fluorescent modes [19], and so on. In existing reports, dual-signal probes are typically composed of at least two materials that enable dual-signal sensing through the inherent properties or the interaction of the materials. However, this requires multiple precursor substances and complex processes.

Nanoenzymes, as synthetic catalysts, have a variety of properties superior to natural enzymes like high stability, cost-effectiveness, and adjustable enzyme activity. Various nanomaterials such as carbon materials [20], metal-based nanomaterials [21], metal–organic framework [22] and two-dimensional nanosheets [23] have been investigated for their enzymatic activities. Carbon dots (CDs), not only possess unique optical properties, but simple synthesis, green raw materials, and cost-effectiveness [24]. Currently, CDs have broad prospects as oxidases [25], peroxidases [25,26] and photo-oxidases [27] in catalytic analysis. Studies have shown that due to the metal ions possessing more outer electrons and larger radii, the spatial distribution and energy gap of the electron density of ion-doped carbon dots (M−CDs) would be changed significantly, so that M−CDs could obtain new physicochemical properties, catalytic activity, for example [28]. Possessing unique optical properties and enzymatic activity, M−CDs have a non-negligible potential for application in the field of dual-mode sensing. The synthesis of most metal-based carbon dots requires the doping of additional metal sources [29,30]. To make the synthesis environmental and easier, we thus chose the only vitamin VB12 containing a metal element as a precursor substance for CDs.

Herein, cobalt-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Co,N-CDs), possessing blue fluorescence emission and oxidase-like activity, were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Co,N-CDs could oxidize the colorless substrate TMB to blue oxTMB, which has absorption peaks at 370 nm and 653 nm. At the same time, FRET occured between the generated oxTMB and Co, N-CDs, and the fluorescence of Co,N-CDs was quenched by oxTMB without the addition of a fluorescence quencher. However, in the presence of GA, oxTMB was re-reduced to colorless TMB, which weakened the FRET interaction between oxTMB and Co,N-CDs, and the fluorescence of Co,N-CDs recovered. In view of the changes of the UV absorption signal at 653 nm and the fluorescence signal at 450 nm, a new colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode detection method for GA was established, and successfully applied to the GA detection in tea beverages.

7100 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), UV2550 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), F200X G2 Transmission Electron Microscope (Tales, USA), FLS980 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK), NICOLET iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

NaCl, MgCl2, MnCl2, L-cysteine, GSH, ZnCl2, KCl, glucose, maltose, galactose, Al2(SO4)3·18H2O, NaAc and ascorbic acid were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), lysine and HAc was acquired from Sinopharm Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The water was ultrapure water with the resistance ≥ 18.25 MΩ∙cm.

Co,N-CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal where VB12 acted as raw material. 0.08 g VB12 was dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 20 min. After completely dissolving, reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling, a 0.22 µm filter was used to remove bulky particulate matter. To remove excess small molecule precursors, the prepared Co,N-CDs were dialyzed in ultrapure water for 24 h in the dark with a dialysis bag (M w = 1000 Da) to gain the final product, and stored at 4 °C in the dark.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry (FT-IR) characterizations were used to analyze the synthesized Co,N-CDs. The distribution and microstructure of Co,N-CDs were observed by TEM. The surface elements and valence states were analyzed by XPS. The FT-IR was used to detect surface functional groups.

100 µL of 0.2 mol/L HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0), 30 µL of Co, N-CDs and 50 µL of TMB solutions with different concentrations were added to 220 µL ultrapure water system, the total volume of the solution was 400 µL. After mixing, the time kinetic curves of the above systems at 653 nm were immediately scanned by UV–Vis spectrophotometer at room temperature to determine the mimetic oxidase activity of Co,N-CDs.

120 µL of ultrapure water, 100 µL of 0.2 mol/L HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4), 30 µL of Co,N-CDs, 50 µL of 6.4 mmol/L TMB solution and 100 µL of GA solution with different concentrations were added in sequence, the total volume of the solution was 400 µL. Then it was placed at 40 °C for 40 min. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was scanned with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, the cuvettes used were quartz cuvettes with a maximum volume of 700 µL, and the absorbance at 653 nm was recorded.

200 µL of the above colorimetric system solution was added to quartz cuvettes with a maximum volume of 350 µL. Under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the fluorescence at 440 nm of the above system was also collected. The negative high voltage of the photomultiplier tube was set to 700 V and the slit was 10 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GA in tea beverages
ce:section.ce:para: Green tea beverage and black tea beverage were used as test samples. After being fully shaken, an appropriate amount of tea samples was taken for centrifugal filtration, and the concentration of GA was detected after diluting 20 times. To verify the accuracy of the method, the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was also used to determine the GA concentration in samples. The flow rate was set to 1 mL/min, the column temperature was 30℃, the injection volume was 20 µL, the mobile phase A was 0.1 % phosphoric acid solution, and the mobile phase B was methanol solution [9].

TEM was adopted to observe the distribution and microscopic morphology of Co,N-CDs (Fig. S1 A). The shape of Co,N-CDs was a regular spherical shape and had satisfactory dispersion in aqueous solution. The inset was the lattice structure diagram of Co,N-CDs, and the lattice spacing was about 0.23 nm. The particle size distribution histogram showed that the size distribution was 2.57 ∼ 4.48 nm (Fig. S1 B), and the average particle size was about 3.42 nm.

To analyze the surface elements, valence states and functional groups of Co,N-CDs, XPS characterization was performed. It could be seen that Co,N-CDs mainly contained four elements, C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and Co 2p, and the corresponding binding energies were 284.8 eV, 531.4 eV, 399.5 eV and 780.6 eV (Fig. 1 A). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s was showed in Fig. 1 B, indicating peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.7 eV, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to C–C/C = C, C–N, and C = O, respectively [31]. The two absorption peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.63 eV observed in the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s belonged to C = O and C-O, respectively (Fig. 1 C). In addition, a characteristic absorption peak appeared at 530.7 eV, which could be attributed to the Co-O chemical bond [32,33]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s was divided into two peaks at 399.4 eV and 400.4 eV (Fig. 1 D), which were the characteristic absorption peaks of amino nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen, respectively, indicating that the surface of Co,N-CDs was rich in amino groups [34]. The high-resolution spectrum of Co 2p had two typical peaks at 780.6 eV and 796.02 eV with satellite peaks (at 785.79 eV and 801.23 eV), which were related to the electron configurations of Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively, indicating that Divalent cobalt was present in Co,N-CDs (Fig. 1 E)[35].

FT-IR was utilized to characterize the chemical structures of Co,N-CDs. The peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration peaks of O-H and C-N functional groups (Fig. 1 F)[35,36]. The characteristic peak at 2972 cm−1 indicated the existence of the N-H chemical bond [37]. The stretching vibration peak at 1680 cm−1 was attributed to C = O [37,38]. In conclusion, Co,N-CDs contain hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl groups.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were scanned for mastering optical properties of Co,N-CDs. The green line in Fig. 1 G was the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Co,N-CDs, showing an obvious absorption peak at 280 nm, which was related to the π → π* transition of the C = C bond [39].

The fluorescence spectra of Co,N-CDs were also scanned, as shown in Fig. 1 G, and the results showed that the maximum emission wavelength was 440 nm on the premise that the maximum excitation wavelength was 360 nm. Under natural light, the color of Co,N-CDs was light brown, while exhibited blue fluorescence in UV light (inset in Fig. 1 G). In addition, to determine whether Co,N-CDs have excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviors, the fluorescence spectra of Co,N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths were also detected, as shown in Fig. 1 H. In the excitation range from 300 nm to 450 nm, the emission wavelength also red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength, indicating that Co,N-CDs have excitation-dependent fluorescence behaviors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the oxidase-like activity of Co,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the oxidase-like catalytic performance of Co,N-CDs, steady-state kinetic experiments were carried out with TMB as substrate. The relationship between the initial velocity (V) of the enzymatic reaction and the concentration (C) of the substrate TMB fits the typical Michaelis-Menten equation, as shown in Fig. S2 A. When the concentration of TMB was lower than 1 mmol/L, V was proportional to the substrate concentration; when the substrate concentration was 1 mmol/L, V reached the maximum; when it exceeded 1 mmol/L, V no longer increased. The value of K m and V max could be obtained form the Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal equation:
[1/V = K m/V max（1/[S]）+1/V max] (Fig. S2 B) (1).

where V is the initial velocity, K m is the steady-state kinetic parameter Michaelis constant, V max is the maximum reaction rate. After calculation, K m was 3.73 mmol/L and V max was 0.013 mmol/L∙s. Compared with horseradish peroxidase (HRP, K m = 0.43 mmol/L, V max = 0.002 mmol/L∙s)[40], the K m of Co,N-CDs was slightly larger, indicating that the affinity of Co,N-CDs for the substrate was weaker than that of HRP. V max was higher than that of HRP, indicating that the oxidase-like activity of Co,N-CDs was higher than that of natural enzyme HRP.

The mechanism of GA detection based on colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal was shown in Scheme 1 . In this study, VB12 containing cobalt and nitrogen elements was selected to synthesize Co,N-CDs via hydrothermal method, as shown in Scheme 1 A, which exhibited unique photoluminescence properties. Co,N-CDs had oxidase-like activity and could oxidize TMB to blue oxTMB under acidic conditions. The system showed absorption peaks at 370 nm and 653 nm attributed to oxTMB. Owing to the FRET between Co,N-CDs and oxTMB, the fluorescence of Co,N-CDs at 440 nm was quenched. GA, with reducibility, could reduce oxTMB to TMB, resulting in that the blue color of the system gradually became lighter and the absorbance at 653 nm decreased accordingly, meanwhile, the fluorescence at 440 nm recovered (Scheme 1 B). Therefore, a new approach for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal detection of GA in food could be established.

The main mechanisms causing fluorescence quenching are FRET and the internal filtration effect (IFE). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the acceptor, overlapping with the emission spectrum of the fluorescent donor is a prerequisite for the occurrence of FRET, while overlapping with the excitation spectrum or emission spectrum of the fluorescent donor is a precondition for the occurrence of the internal filtration effect (IFE)[41,42]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, curve a was the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oxTMB with two characteristic absorption peaks at 370 nm and 653 nm. Curve b was the excitation spectrum of Co,N-CDs, and curve c was the emission spectrum of Co,N-CDs, showing a maximum excitation of 360 nm and a maximum emission of 440 nm. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oxTMB overlapped with both the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of Co,N-CDs, which was consistent with the spectral overlap characteristic of FRET and IFE. Therefore, it suggests that FRET or IFE may have occurred leading to fluorescence quenching of Co,N-CDs. However, besides spectral overlap, another direct evidence for distinguishing FRET or IFE is the fluorescence lifetime of the donor. For the donor molecules, the occurrence of FRET is accompanied by the decay of their fluorescence lifetime, while no change in fluorescence lifetime during IFE occurrence. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of Co,N-CDs in the absence and presence of oxTMB was measured. The results were shown in Fig. 2 B, in the absence of oxTMB, the average fluorescence lifetime of Co,N-CDs was 5.98 ns, whereas the average lifetime decreased to 4.76 ns in the presence of oxTMB. The average fluorescence lifetime decayed by 20.4 % compared to without oxTMB. Thus, possessing the two characteristics of FRET, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oxTMB overlapped with the emission spectrum of Co,N-CDs and the decay of fluorescence lifetime of Co,N-CDs, illustrating that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was the occurrence of FRET between oxTMB and Co,N-CDs.

Since the type of buffer, pH, amount of Co,N-CDs and TMB, temperature and time of the reaction all affect the enzymatic reaction, it was necessary to optimize. The results were displayed in the Fig. S3-8. The optimal reaction conditions were that 30 µL Co,N-CDs were reacted with 0.8 mmol/L TMB in NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4) for 40 min at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard curves, detection limits and precision
ce:section.ce:para: With the addition of GA, the absorbance of the system gradually decreased (Fig. 3 A). As shown in Fig. 3 B, in the concentration range of 0.1 ∼ 15 μmol/L, ΔA had a linear relationship with C, the linear equation was as follows:

where ΔA is the change in absorbance value before and after the addition of GA, C is the concentration of GA, and r is the correlation coefficient. The detection limit was 32.01 nmol/L based on the following equation:
LOD = 3σ/slope (3).

where σ is standard deviation. In addition, GA standard solutions with low, medium and high concentrations (0.1, 5, and 15 μmol/L) were selected for precision detection. After seven parallel experiments, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 4.07 %, 2.17 %, and 3.55 %, respectively, suggesting that the colorimetric method has satisfactory precision.

The presence of GA reduced the quenching effect of oxTMB on Co,N-CDs, thus restoring the fluorescence. The fluorescence of the system was recovered with the increase of GA concentration (Fig. 3 C). The standard curve for GA detection via fluorescence method was shown in Fig. 3 D. In the concentration range of 0.01 ∼ 15 μmol/L, the equation of fluorometric method was as follows:

where ΔF defines as the recovery value of the fluorescence intensity before and after adding GA, C is the concentration of GA, and r is the correlation coefficient. The detection limit was 1.54 nmol/L based on equation (3). The RSDs obtained by 7 parallel determinations of low, medium and high concentrations of GA (0.01, 5, 15 μmol/L) were 4.05 %, 4.24 % and 3.84 %, respectively, indicating that the method has good repeatability.

Selectivity is critical to the practical application of analytical methods. In order to explore the selectivity of the dual-signal model for GA sensing, the interference that may be contained in beverages such as ions, sugars, and amino acids on GA detection was investigated. The effects of 19 interfering substances in the colorimetric method were shown in Fig. 4 A. The relative error was controlled within ± 5 %, 1000 times of Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, HCO3 –, CO3 2–, Cl- and trisodium citrate, 500 times of Zn2+, glycine and glucose, 400 times of K+, maltose and galactose, 20 times of Al3+, 16 times of riboflavin and lysine, and 2 times of ascorbic acid had negligible effects on the detection of GA (5 μmol/L). Furthermore, the addition of the sulfhydryl masking agent N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) could eliminate the effects of L-cysteine (L-cys) and glutathione (GSH) on GA detection.

The interference of the above interference on the fluorometric method was presented in Fig. 4 B. 1000 times of K+, Na+, Zn2+, CO3 2–, Cl-, maltose, galactose and glycine, 500 times of Mg2+, glucose and trisodium citrate, 200 times of HCO3 –, 50 times of Mn2+, 40 times of Al3+, 8 times of riboflavin and lysine, 2 times of ascorbic acid did not cause interference with GA detection. NEM also could eliminate the effects of L-cys and GSH. Therefore, the approach for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal detection of GA established in this paper has excellent selectivity.

In order to evaluate the practicability of the method, the GA contents in two tea beverages was determined, and recovery experiment was also carried out. The results in Table 1 demonstrated that the spiked recoveries were 96.44 %∼103.48 % for colorimetric method and 96.38 %∼104.05 % for fluorometric method. In addition, the standard method HPLC was used to further verify the accuracy of the method for detecting GA (Fig S9). The established colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal detection method of GA was consistent with the detection results of HPLC (GA in green tea and black tea was 4.39 μmol/L and 3.29 μmol/L, respectively). Therefore, the novel based on oxidase-like activity constructed in this study has a good application prospect for GA detection in tea beverages.

In conclusion, a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode system for GA detection was established based on the photoluminescence property and oxidase-like activity of Co,N-CDs. Co,N-CDs could oxidize the chromogenic substrate TMB to blue oxTMB, resulting in the absorption peaks of oxTMB at 370 nm and 653 nm, and the fluorescence of Co,N-CDs was quenched by oxTMB. The presence of GA reduced the absorbance of the system and restored the fluorescence of Co,N-CDs by inhibiting the oxidation of Co,N-CDs. Thus, a new method for colorimetric and fluorometric dual-signal detection of GA in tea beverages was established. The as-prepared probe is convenient to synthesize and environmental, and the sensing method achieves complementary advantages to a certain extent like reducing the detection limit and ensuring the accuracy of the method. This method has preliminary applications for the detection of GA in tea beverages, which is significance for the evaluation of food safety.

Congcong Hu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yu Yin: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Validation. Chongyang Huang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Yaolin Dong: Formal analysis. Jinquan Liu: Formal analysis. Fubing Xiao: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision. Shengyuan Yang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.82073604, 21906077), Postgraduate Research and Innovation Project of Hunan Province (CX20210962).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109735.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
